lines.speaker: BEDFORD
lines.text: ’Fore God, his grace is bold, to trust these traitors.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: They shall be apprehended by and by.

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: How smooth and even they do bear themselves!

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: As if allegiance in their bosoms sat,

lines.speaker: WESTMORELAND
lines.text: Crowned with faith and constant loyalty.

lines.speaker: BEDFORD
lines.text: The king hath note of all that they intend,

lines.speaker: BEDFORD
lines.text: By interception which they dream not of.

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: Nay, but the man that was his bedfellow,

lines.speaker: EXETER

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: That he should, for a foreign purse, so sell

lines.speaker: EXETER

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Now sits the wind fair, and we will aboard.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My Lord of Cambridge, and my kind Lord of Masham,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And you, my gentle knight, give me your thoughts:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY

lines.speaker: KING HENRY

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Doing the execution and the act

lines.speaker: KING HENRY

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: No doubt, my liege, if each man do his best.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I doubt not that; since we are well persuaded

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We carry not a heart with us from hence

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That grows not in a fair consent with ours,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Nor leave not one behind that doth not wish

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Success and conquest to attend on us.

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: Never was monarch better fear’d and loved

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: Than is your majesty: there’s not, I think, a subject

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: That sits in heart-grief and uneasiness

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: Under the sweet shade of your government.

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: True: those that were your father’s enemies

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: Have steep’d their galls in honey and do serve you

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: With hearts create of duty and of zeal.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We therefore have great cause of thankfulness;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And shall forget the office of our hand,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Sooner than quittance of desert and merit

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: According to the weight and worthiness.

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: So service shall with steeled sinews toil,

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: And labour shall refresh itself with hope,

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: To do your grace incessant services.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We judge no less. Uncle of Exeter,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Enlarge the man committed yesterday,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That rail’d against our person: we consider

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: It was excess of wine that set him on;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And on his more advice we pardon him.

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: That’s mercy, but too much security:

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: Let him be punish’d, sovereign, lest example

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: Breed, by his sufferance, more of such a kind.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O, let us yet be merciful.

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: So may your highness, and yet punish too.

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: Sir,

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: You show great mercy, if you give him life,

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: After the taste of much correction.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Alas, your too much love and care of me

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Are heavy orisons ’gainst this poor wretch!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If little faults, proceeding on distemper,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall not be wink’d at, how shall we stretch our eye

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: When capital crimes, chew’d, swallow’d and digested,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Appear before us? We’ll yet enlarge that man,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Though Cambridge, Scroop and Grey, in their dear care

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And tender preservation of our person,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Would have him punish’d. And now to our French causes:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Who are the late commissioners?

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: I one, my lord:

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: Your highness bade me ask for it today.

lines.speaker: SCROOP
lines.text: So did you me, my liege.

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: And I, my royal sovereign.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then, Richard Earl of Cambridge, there is yours;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Grey of Northumberland, this same is yours:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Read them; and know, I know your worthiness.

lines.text: My Lord of Westmoreland, and uncle Exeter,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We will aboard tonight. Why, how now, gentlemen!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What see you in those papers that you lose

lines.text: So much complexion? Look ye, how they change!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Their cheeks are paper. Why, what read you there,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That have so cowarded and chased your blood

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Out of appearance?

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: I do confess my fault;

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: And do submit me to your highness’ mercy.

lines.speaker: GREY AND SCROOP
lines.text: To which we all appeal.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The mercy that was quick in us but late,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: By your own counsel is suppress’d and kill’d:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: You must not dare, for shame, to talk of mercy;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For your own reasons turn into your bosoms,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As dogs upon their masters, worrying you.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: See you, my princes and my noble peers,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: These English monsters! My Lord of Cambridge here,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: You know how apt our love was to accord

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To furnish him with an appertinents

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Belonging to his honour; and this man

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Hath, for a few light crowns, lightly conspired,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And sworn unto the practices of France,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To kill us here in Hampton: to the which

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: This knight, no less for bounty bound to us

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Than Cambridge is, hath likewise sworn. But, O,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What shall I say to thee, Lord Scroop? thou cruel,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Ingrateful, savage and inhuman creature!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Thou that didst bear the key of all my counsels,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That knew’st the very bottom of my soul,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That almost mightst have coin’d me into gold,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Wouldst thou have practised on me for thy use!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: May it be possible, that foreign hire

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Could out of thee extract one spark of evil

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That might annoy my finger? ’tis so strange,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That, though the truth of it stands off as gross

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As black and white, my eye will scarcely see it.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Treason and murder ever kept together,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: As two yoke-devils sworn to either’s purpose,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Working so grossly in a natural cause,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That admiration did not whoop at them:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But thou, ’gainst all proportion, didst bring in

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Wonder to wait on treason and on murder:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And whatsoever cunning fiend it was

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That wrought upon thee so preposterously

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Hath got the voice in hell for excellence:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And other devils that suggest by treasons

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Do botch and bungle up damnation

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With patches, colours and with forms being fetch’d

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: From glistering semblances of piety;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But he that temper’d thee bade thee stand up,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Gave thee no instance why thou shouldst do treason,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Unless to dub thee with the name of traitor.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If that same demon that hath gull’d thee thus

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Should with his lion gait walk the whole world,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: He might return to vasty Tartar back,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And tell the legions “I can never win

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: A soul so easy as that Englishman’s.”

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O, how hast thou with jealousy infected

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The sweetness of affiance! Show men dutiful?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Why, so didst thou: seem they grave and learned?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Why, so didst thou: come they of noble family?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Why, so didst thou: seem they religious?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Why, so didst thou: or are they spare in diet,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Free from gross passion or of mirth or anger,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Constant in spirit, not swerving with the blood,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Garnish’d and deck’d in modest complement,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Not working with the eye without the ear,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And but in purged judgement trusting neither?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Such and so finely bolted didst thou seem:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And thus thy fall hath left a kind of blot,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To mark the full-fraught man and best indued

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With some suspicion. I will weep for thee;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For this revolt of thine, methinks, is like

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Another fall of man. Their faults are open:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Arrest them to the answer of the law;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And God acquit them of their practices!

lines.speaker: EXETER
lines.text: I arrest thee of high treason, by the name of Richard Earl of Cambridge.

lines.speaker: EXETER

lines.text: Our purposes God justly hath discover’d;

lines.speaker: SCROOP

lines.speaker: SCROOP

lines.speaker: SCROOP

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: For me, the gold of France did not seduce;

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: Which I in sufferance heartily will rejoice,

lines.speaker: CAMBRIDGE
lines.text: Beseeching God and you to pardon me.

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: Never did faithful subject more rejoice

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: At the discovery of most dangerous treason

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: Than I do at this hour joy o’er myself,

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: Prevented from a damned enterprise:

lines.speaker: GREY
lines.text: My fault, but not my body, pardon, sovereign.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: God quit you in his mercy! Hear your sentence.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: You have conspired against our royal person,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Join’d with an enemy proclaim’d and from his coffers

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Received the golden earnest of our death;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Wherein you would have sold your king to slaughter,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: His princes and his peers to servitude,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: His subjects to oppression and contempt

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And his whole kingdom into desolation.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Touching our person seek we no revenge;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But we our kingdom’s safety must so tender,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whose ruin you have sought, that to her laws

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We do deliver you. Get you therefore hence,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Poor miserable wretches, to your death:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The taste whereof, God of his mercy give

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: You patience to endure, and true repentance

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Of all your dear offences! Bear them hence.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Now, lords, for France; the enterprise whereof

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall be to you, as us, like glorious.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We doubt not of a fair and lucky war,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Since God so graciously hath brought to light

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: This dangerous treason lurking in our way

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To hinder our beginnings. We doubt not now

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But every rub is smoothed on our way.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then forth, dear countrymen: let us deliver

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Our puissance into the hand of God,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Putting it straight in expedition.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Cheerly to sea; the signs of war advance:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No king of England, if not king of France.

stage_directions.text: Trumpets sound. Enter King Henry, Scroop, Cambridge, Grey, and Attendants.
Exeunt Cambridge, Scroop and Grey, guarded.

stamped me as a coin — used me as a mint for his own enrichment

glosses.definition: adorned and decorated with modest bearing

glosses.phrase: No king of England, if not king of France
glosses.definition: if I can't be king of France, I'm not really king of England — Henry's aggressive closing oath

annotations.title: The trap sprung — Henry's performance of mercy
annotations.body: Henry stages the traitors' entrapment with theatrical care. He first proposes releasing a drunken man who railed against him; the three traitors pile on insisting the king be harsher; Henry gently overrules them and grants mercy. Only then does he hand them the papers that, as they read them, reveal their own treason. The trap's elegance is the point: the traitors have just demanded strict punishment, and Henry will now apply to them the logic they themselves urged. 'The mercy that was quick in us but late, / By your own counsel is suppress'd and kill'd' (lines 77–78). This is the kind of monarch Henry has become: one who makes his enemies condemn themselves with their own argument. Readers may recall Prince Hal in Henry IV Part 2, who famously banishes Falstaff at his coronation with calculated public timing. The political method is the same. What looks like improvised royal authority is in fact carefully rehearsed dramaturgy.

annotations.title: Scroop and the Second Fall
annotations.body: Henry's long reproach to Scroop in particular — 'Thou that didst bear the key of all my counsels' — is the most emotionally charged thing Henry says in the play up to this point. Scroop was his intimate, his 'bedfellow' in the period's sense of confidant. His betrayal is framed as 'another fall of man,' a second Eden. The theological vocabulary is deliberate: Henry's scandal is not merely political but cosmological. A trusted companion who was 'spare in diet, / Free from gross passion,' 'garnish'd and deck'd in modest complement' — exactly the virtues Canterbury earlier attributed to Henry himself in 1.1 — has proven hollow. The passage resonates with the Henry IV plays' preoccupation with false appearances and feigned loyalty. It is also the moment the play comes closest to expressing genuine anguish from Henry; he announces it himself ('I will weep for thee'). The anguish is rhetorical as well as felt; it is the only proper setting in which to pronounce a capital sentence against a man who once ate at your table.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

trials.prompt: Why does Henry first raise (and then grant) mercy for the drunkard before exposing the traitors?
options: Because he has forgotten about the traitors
To make the traitors commit themselves to strict punishment — using their own advocacy for severity against them when their treason is revealed
Because the drunk was an old friend

trials.prompt: Henry's reproach to Scroop frames the betrayal as 'another fall of man.' Why this theological framing?
options: Because Shakespeare needs to fill time
Because the betrayal of trusted intimacy is figured as a second Eden — a cosmic scandal, not just a political one. It also echoes the Henry IV plays' preoccupation with false appearances
Because the Salic Law speech required biblical balance

trials.prompt: What does the scene end with?
options: Henry sparing the traitors at the last moment
A verdict of death for all three traitors, and Henry's immediate sailing to France ('No king of England, if not king of France')
A council deciding to delay the French invasion
